topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was oppressive, thick enough to choke on. Elias traced the dust motes dancing in the single shaft of afternoon sun cutting across his desk. He kept glancing at the framed photograph on the corner of his monitor—a smiling man with kind eyes, his mentor, Arthur. Arthur had simply vanished. No note, no call, just an empty chair where the presence used to be. Elias pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding unnaturally loud in the quiet expanse. He picked up the heavy, leather-bound journal Arthur had always insisted he keep, the spine feeling strangely brittle under his fingertips. A hollow ache settled deep in his chest, a heavy weight that made breathing shallow. He reread the last entry, the ink seeming to mock him with its finality.

[story 2]

When Sarah walked into the study, the air seemed to drain from the room. She stood by the window, her shoulders slumped as if carrying an invisible burden. She hadn't seen him. Arthur’s office was immaculate, unnervingly so, every award neatly aligned, every stack of papers perfectly stacked. It was the perfect picture of a life neatly concluded, a life that had abruptly stopped mid-sentence. Sarah pressed her palms against the cool glass, her knuckles turning white. "He's gone," she whispered, the sound barely disturbing the stillness. The realization wasn't a sharp shock, but a slow, cold seep that permeated her bones, leaving her feeling strangely numb and heavy, like wading through deep, undisturbed water.

[story 3]

Mark stared at the closed door, the wood feeling cold and unresponsive beneath his fingertips. He had waited by the reception desk for nearly an hour, the clock ticking with agonizing slowness. Every passing minute stretched out, thick and resistant. When the assistant finally emerged, her expression was professionally neutral, utterly devoid of warmth. "Mr. Vance has stepped down," she stated, her voice flat, as if reading a grocery list. Mark felt a sudden, profound deflation, a sudden loss of buoyancy. He didn't move; he simply stood there, his gaze fixed on the polished floor, the energy draining out of him until he felt strangely light, yet profoundly empty, as if his own internal reserves had been completely depleted.
